games.explanation: The market is treating this like a Yankees home game against a bad team, and one of those things is true. Ryan Weathers is a soft-contact lefty whose numbers against righties look a lot like batting practice for a lineup that leads with James Wood, and Wood has been doing violent things to right-handed pitching all year. Palmquist is the wart on this ticket, but the Nationals only need to hang around against a starter surrendering hard contact on nearly half the balls in play. Getting plus money on the better Elo team is the kind of pricing error that shows up when a logo does the handicapping. +137 on the team the math actually likes.

Hunter Greene misses bats, but the Cubs are built exactly wrong for him, stacked with lefties who've been squaring him up all season while a strikeout artist gets priced like an ace. Greene's numbers against left-handed bats crater to a .307 xwOBA with a 58.8% hard-hit rate, and Chicago rolls out Busch, PCA, and Suzuki to feast on that specific weakness. Imanaga isn't Greene's equal on stuff, but he throws strikes and gets a night with the wind blowing in, which quietly neutralizes the Great American launchpad. At -110, you're getting the better team, the better lineup matchup, and a park that just lost its teeth.

Parker Messick has faced 165 big-league hitters and the market is already treating him like a known quantity, which is exactly the kind of leap that gets punished by a Miami lineup swinging from the correct side. Stowers in particular is a nightmare draw for a rookie lefty, running a .395 xwOBA against righties that suggests he handles this profile with room to spare, and Lopez and Hernandez round out a righty-heavy top of the order built to sting Messick's flatter side. Alcantara is not vintage, but he does not have to be against a Guardians lineup missing thump and leaning on unproven bats. Miami is the better team by a healthy margin here, and -119 is asking you to pay flat money on the side that should be favored more clearly than that.

Nolan McLean's numbers look shiny in the aggregate, but the split that matters here is the one where he stops missing bats and starts issuing free passes. Against righties he's been distinctly hittable, and Boston happens to run a right-handed heavy top of the order featuring Abreu and Romy Gonzalez, exactly the barrel-first profile that punishes command wobble. Sonny Gray isn't ace-tier anymore but he's the steadier arm in this matchup by a fair margin, and a plus price on the better team plus the better starter is the whole story. At +110, the market is pricing this like a coin flip when it's closer to a lean.

Hunter Brown is a legitimate frontline arm, but the market is pricing this like Houston is the far better team and they simply aren't. These two are a coin flip on Elo, the projected score is basically a tie, and Texas rolls out a top of the order with real thump against righties in Langford, Nimmo, and Pederson. Brown's numbers are pristine but they come with a walk rate north of nine percent, which is exactly how a Quantrill start stays close enough for the bats to steal one. Getting plus money on the team the math likes is the whole ballgame.

Chris Sale is having a Chris Sale season, which is the entire reason this line exists, but the Cardinals happen to bring a lineup tilted toward the one thing he doesn't crush. Herrera in particular has been a different hitter against lefties, posting a .451 xwOBA in that split, and Walker's contact quality plays up when the platoon flips too. Leahy isn't Sale, but he doesn't have to be against an Albies-and-Harris core that keeps grading out softer than the names suggest, with only Olson looking like a real problem. Getting a plus-money price on a dog that matches up cleanly with the ace across the diamond is the trade you make every time. +141 on a coin flip is doing plenty of work here.

At +110, the market is pricing this like a coin flip when it's closer to a lean.

Chicago being laid as a run-and-a-half favorite requires believing Sean Burke is going to shut down a lineup he has no business shutting down, and the walk column alone should give you pause. Burke is issuing free passes to nearly 12 percent of the hitters he faces, and Nick Kurtz specifically has been demolishing right-handed pitching to the tune of a .423 xwOBA. Meanwhile Jacob Lopez quietly runs a sub-.300 xwOBA-against with strikeout stuff, which is not the profile of a guy who gets blown off the mound in a bullpen game. The sim has this landing inside a run, and getting a hook and a half at this number is the entire pitch.

Hunter Brown is a legitimate frontline arm, but the market is pricing this like Houston is the far better team and they simply aren't. These two are a coin flip on Elo, the projected score is basically a tie, and Texas rolls out a top of the order with real thump against righties in Langford, Nimmo, and Pederson. Brown's numbers are pristine but they come with a walk rate north of nine percent, which is exactly how a Quantrill start stays close enough for the bats to steal one. Getting plus money on the team the math likes is the whole ballgame.

Chris Sale is having a Chris Sale season, which is the entire reason this line exists, but the Cardinals happen to bring a lineup tilted toward the one thing he doesn't crush. Herrera in particular has been a different hitter against lefties, posting a .451 xwOBA in that split, and Walker's contact quality plays up when the platoon flips too. Leahy isn't Sale, but he doesn't have to be against an Albies-and-Harris core that keeps grading out softer than the names suggest, with only Olson looking like a real problem. Getting a plus-money price on a dog that matches up cleanly with the ace across the diamond is the trade you make every time. +141 on a coin flip is doing plenty of work here.

Getting a full run and a plus price against Ohtani feels like a trap until you notice Arizona's lineup is built almost entirely of right-handed bats, which is the only side of the plate where he looks mortal. Ohtani has been dominant overall, but righties have tagged him for a wOBA nearly ninety points higher than lefties, and Marte, Perdomo, and Carroll are exactly the kind of contact-plus-thump profiles that don't chase. Add in a park that plays small for home runs and a forecast that won't help anyone command a slider, and the blowout script the market is pricing gets a lot harder to write. Rodriguez isn't winning any Cy Youngs, but the sim has this inside a run for a reason. At even money on the runline, you only need Arizona to keep it a game.
